findings.description: Header seal/logo graphics are missing from the HTML rendering.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the PDF shows the North Carolina state seal at the top center and a DEQ logo near the bottom left. In the HTML, these graphics are absent and only text remains.

findings.description: Header typography and emphasis are not fully preserved.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the PDF presents the left header names/titles and the centered agency heading with stronger visual hierarchy and italicized titles, while the HTML renders them more uniformly with reduced distinction.

findings.description: Signature image is missing from the HTML rendering.
findings.evidence: On page 2, the PDF includes a handwritten signature above 'Judith A. Wehner'. In the HTML, only the typed name/title appear with no signature graphic.

findings.description: Underline/bold styling is only partially preserved.
findings.evidence: On page 2, 'Certified Mail' is underlined in the PDF and the salutation/closing area has stronger visual formatting. In the HTML, some styling appears flatter and less distinct, though text remains readable.

findings.description: HTML heading structure does not reflect the document structure; no semantic headings are used despite clear letterhead/title-like sections in the PDF, and the HTML nests multiple <main> elements instead.
findings.evidence:
PDF shows distinct structural elements such as 'NORTH CAROLINA Environmental Quality', date line, 'RE: Proposed Alamance Quarry and Construction Materials Quarry', and page-two continuation. HTML uses only

tags and repeated

containers with no h1-h6 headings.

findings.description: Certified mail tracking number spacing differs from the PDF, altering the exact text string.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows: '7008 1300 00001126 1372'. HTML page 1 shows: '7008 1300 00001126 1372' appears to match visually except the PDF spacing is tied to the scanned line; however, the HTML omits the visual separation/formatting exactly as scanned. This is a very minor textual normalization, not a substantive error.
